content:
{
  "title": "FactChain: Chained Fact Retrieval Prompts for Reducing LLM Hallucinations",
  "problem": "Large language models often hallucinate facts, producing confident but inaccurate outputs. Existing prompting strategies insufficiently constrain factuality in open-domain generation.",
  "motivation": "Factual inaccuracies undermine LLM reliability, especially in domains like science, medicine, or history. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes and knowledge-intensive tasks, effective, lightweight methods to enforce factual accuracy at inference time are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose FactChain prompting: before answering a user query, the LLM is prompted to generate a chain of explicit, verifiable factual assertions relevant to the question, and then required to ground its final answer only in those asserted facts. The prompt template explicitly separates the 'fact generation' and 'answer synthesis' steps, and instructs the model to refrain from using information not present in its preceding fact chain. This structured prompting constrains generation to verifiable content, reducing the chance of hallucination. Optionally, external verification (e.g., retrieval-augmented self-checking) can be incorporated to further prune unsupported facts in the chain.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate FactChain on open-domain QA datasets (e.g., HotpotQA, TriviaQA) measuring hallucination rates, factual consistency, and answer accuracy. Baselines will include standard zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting, and retrieval-augmented generation. Metrics will include human and automated factuality judgments and standard QA accuracy. The key experiment will measure hallucination reduction and answer correctness against the strongest baseline (retrieval-augmented CoT) on held-out QA tasks."
}
